docs.title: Calegari Group Tutorials
docs.text: Welcome to the group wiki. Here you will find tutorials and reference guides for the codes and tools we use daily.

docs.title: Contents
docs.text: Section Description Installation How to compile and install codes on HPC clusters LAMMPS Input file structure and usage examples Quantum ESPRESSO Input file structure for DFT calculations VASP Input files (INCAR, POSCAR, KPOINTS, POTCAR) Python & ASE Python basics and ASE for atomistic simulations HPC Job schedulers, submission scripts, cluster tips

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
If you are new to the group, start with the Installation section for the machine you are working on, then move to the code-specific tutorials.

Contributing

To add or improve a tutorial, open a pull request on the GitHub repository.

docs.title: Contributing to the Wiki
docs.text: This page explains how to add or edit tutorials so that the wiki stays up to date.

docs.title: How the wiki works
docs.text:
The wiki is built with MkDocs Material. You write plain Markdown files inside the docs/ folder, and MkDocs converts them into the website. The source lives on GitHub (marcoscaa/group-wiki) and the live site is served via GitHub Pages.

group-wiki/
├── mkdocs.yml        ← site config: theme, navigation, extensions
└── docs/
    ├── index.md      ← home page
    ├── installation/
    ├── lammps/
    ├── qe/
    ├── vasp/
    ├── python/
    ├── hpc/
    └── contributing/ ← you are here

docs.title: Setup (first time only)
docs.text:
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone git@github.com:marcoscaa/group-wiki.git
cd group-wiki

# 2. Install MkDocs Material
pip install mkdocs-material

# 3. Preview the site locally
python3 -m mkdocs serve
# Open http://127.0.0.1:8000 in your browser

The local preview auto-reloads every time you save a file — no need to restart.

docs.title: Edit an existing page
docs.text: Open any .md file under docs/ in your text editor and make your changes. The file path mirrors the URL: docs/lammps/input_file.md → .../lammps/input_file/.

docs.title: Add a new page
docs.text:
1. Create a new .md file in the appropriate subfolder: 

    docs/lammps/thermostats.md

  2. Register it in mkdocs.yml under the nav: section: 

    nav:
  - LAMMPS:
    - Overview: lammps/index.md
    - Input File Structure: lammps/input_file.md
    - Thermostats: lammps/thermostats.md   # ← add this line

docs.title: Add a new section
docs.text:
1. Create a new subfolder and an index.md inside it: 

    docs/new_topic/index.md

  2. Add the section to mkdocs.yml: 

    nav:
  - New Topic:
    - Overview: new_topic/index.md

docs.title: Markdown reference
docs.text: All pages are written in standard Markdown with a few useful extensions enabled.

docs.title: Headings
docs.text:
# Page title (H1 — one per page)
## Major section (H2)
### Subsection (H3)

docs.title: Code blocks
docs.text:
Specify the language for syntax highlighting:

```bash
module load python/3.10.8
source venv/bin/activate
```

```python
import numpy as np
a = np.array([1, 2, 3])
```

```fortran
&SYSTEM
  nat = 2
  ecutwfc = 60.0
/
```

```lammps
units    metal
pair_style mace no_domain_decomposition
run      10000
```

docs.title: Admonitions (callout boxes)
docs.text:
!!! tip "Optional title"
    Use this for helpful hints.

!!! note
    Use this for extra context or reminders.

!!! warning
    Use this for common pitfalls or important caveats.

Renders as:

Optional title

Use this for helpful hints.

Warning

Use this for common pitfalls or important caveats.

docs.title: Tabbed content
docs.text:
Useful for showing the same steps on different machines or systems:

=== "Tuolumne (LLNL)"
    ```bash
    module load rocm/6.2.4hangfix
    ```

=== "Another cluster"
    ```bash
    module load cuda/12.0
    ```

docs.title: Tables
docs.text:
| Column A | Column B | Column C |
|---|---|---|
| value 1  | value 2  | value 3  |

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
[link text](other_page.md)          # internal link
[link text](../lammps/index.md)     # link to another section
[LAMMPS website](https://lammps.org) # external link

docs.title: Publishing your changes
docs.text:
Once you are happy with the edits:

# 1. Stage and commit the source files
git add docs/ mkdocs.yml
git commit -m "Brief description of what you added or changed"
git push

# 2. Deploy the live site to GitHub Pages
python3 -m mkdocs gh-deploy

The live site updates within about a minute after gh-deploy.

Never commit the site/ folder manually

The site/ folder is generated automatically by gh-deploy. Do not git add site/ — it is managed separately on the gh-pages branch.

docs.title: Workflow summary
docs.text:
edit docs/*.md
      ↓
python3 -m mkdocs serve   (preview at localhost:8000)
      ↓
git add + git commit + git push   (save source to GitHub)
      ↓
python3 -m mkdocs gh-deploy       (publish to live site)

docs.title: HPC Overview
docs.text: Tips and reference guides for working on HPC clusters.

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text: Guide Description Flux Job Scheduler Submitting and monitoring jobs with Flux

docs.title: Flux Job Scheduler
docs.text: Flux is the workload manager used on Frontier (ORNL).

docs.title: Submission script template
docs.text:
#!/bin/sh
#FLUX: --job-name=example
#FLUX: --output='flux.{{id}}.out'
#FLUX: --error='flux.{{id}}.err'
#FLUX: -N 1
#FLUX: --time-limit=24h
#FLUX: --bank=pr4ccc
#FLUX: --queue=pbatch

echo "Starting job at $(date)"

CORES_PER_TASK="${CORES_PER_TASK:-24}"

flux run -N 1 -n 1 \
  --cores-per-task="${CORES_PER_TASK}" \
  --gpus-per-task=4 \
  YOUR_CODE_HERE

echo "Finishing job at $(date)"

docs.title: Key directives
docs.text: Directive Meaning -N Number of nodes --time-limit Wall-clock limit (e.g. 24h, 30m) --bank Allocation account --queue Partition/queue name --cores-per-task CPU cores assigned per MPI rank --gpus-per-task GPUs assigned per MPI rank

docs.title: Common commands
docs.text:
# Submit a job
flux batch my_script.sh

# Check job status
flux jobs

# Cancel a job
flux cancel <jobid>

# View job output in real time
flux job attach <jobid>

# Check allocation/bank info
flux account info

docs.title: Running interactively
docs.text:
flux alloc -N 1 --time-limit=1h --bank=pr4ccc --queue=pbatch

This drops you into an interactive shell on the allocated node.

docs.title: Installation Overview
docs.text: This section covers how to install and compile the codes used in the group on HPC clusters.

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text: Guide Target machine Notes MACE Tuolumne/LLNL (AMD GPU) Standalone Python install MACE + LAMMPS Tuolumne/LLNL (AMD GPU) Full ML-MD stack with Kokkos/HIP

docs.title: General Tips
docs.text:
Virtual environments

Always install Python packages inside a virtual environment (python -m venv). This keeps your installs isolated and reproducible across group members.

Module conflicts

Run module purge before loading any modules to avoid conflicts from previously loaded environments.

docs.title: Install MACE (standalone)
docs.text: This guide installs the MACE machine-learning interatomic potential framework on a machine with AMD GPUs (ROCm), such as Tuolumne at LLNL.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Access to an AMD GPU node
  • The following modules available: python/3.10.8, PrgEnv-gnu, gcc/12.2.1-magic, craype-accel-amd-gfx942, cray-mpich/9.0.1, cmake/3.24.2, rocm/6.2.4hangfix

docs.title: 1. Load modules
docs.text:
module purge
module load python/3.10.8
module load PrgEnv-gnu gcc/12.2.1-magic craype-accel-amd-gfx942 cray-mpich/9.0.1 cmake/3.24.2 rocm/6.2.4hangfix
export CC=mpicc
export CXX=hipcc

docs.title: 2. Create and activate a virtual environment
docs.text:
python -m venv venvs/mace_env
source venvs/mace_env/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel

docs.title: 3. Install PyTorch with ROCm support
docs.text:
pip install torch==2.6.0 torchvision==0.21.0 torchaudio==2.6.0 \
    --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/rocm6.2.4

docs.title: 4. Install MACE
docs.text:
pip install cython pybind11
git clone https://github.com/ACEsuit/mace.git
pip install ./mace

docs.title: Verification
docs.text:
import mace
import torch
print("MACE version:", mace.__version__)
print("CUDA/ROCm available:", torch.cuda.is_available())

docs.title: Install MACE + LAMMPS (with Kokkos/HIP)
docs.text: This guide builds LAMMPS with the PKG_ML-MACE and Kokkos/HIP packages for AMD GPU acceleration, using the symmetrix pair style. Target machine: Tuolumne at LLNL (AMD MI300A, gfx942).

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Access to a GPU node on Tuolumne
  • The following modules available: StdEnv, python/3.10.8, PrgEnv-gnu, gcc/12.2.1-magic, craype-accel-amd-gfx942, cray-mpich/9.0.1, cmake/3.29.2, rocm/6.2.4hangfix

docs.title: Full install script
docs.text:
You can run the steps below manually, or save them as a script and execute it from your working directory.

#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail

ROOT="$PWD"

docs.title: 1. Load modules and set compilers
docs.text:
module --force purge
module load StdEnv
module load python/3.10.8
module load PrgEnv-gnu
module load gcc/12.2.1-magic
module load craype-accel-amd-gfx942
module load cray-mpich/9.0.1
module load cmake/3.29.2
module load rocm/6.2.4hangfix

export CC=mpicc
export CXX=hipcc
export KOKKOS_HOST_COMPILER=mpicxx
export CRAYPE_LINK_TYPE=dynamic

docs.title: 2. Create virtual environment and install dependencies
docs.text:
mkdir -p "${ROOT}/venvs"
python -m venv "${ROOT}/venvs/allegro_env"
source "${ROOT}/venvs/allegro_env/bin/activate"

python -m pip install --upgrade pip setuptools wheel

pip install torch==2.6.0 torchvision==0.21.0 torchaudio==2.6.0 \
    --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/rocm6.2.4

pip install cython pybind11 scikit-build-core ninja

docs.title: 3. Install MACE
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/ACEsuit/mace.git
pip install ./mace

docs.title: 4. Clone LAMMPS and symmetrix
docs.text:
mkdir -p "${ROOT}/mylammps"
cd "${ROOT}/mylammps"

git clone -b release https://github.com/lammps/lammps
git clone --recursive https://github.com/wcwitt/symmetrix

docs.title: 5. Find CMake paths (helper)
docs.text:
Run this inside your activated environment to confirm the correct paths:

python - <<'PY'
import torch, pybind11, pathlib, site
print("Torch CMake:", pathlib.Path(torch.__file__).parent / "share/cmake")
print("pybind11 CMake:", pathlib.Path(pybind11.__file__).parent / "share/cmake/pybind11")
print("Python site-packages:", site.getsitepackages())
PY

docs.title: 6. Install symmetrix into LAMMPS source
docs.text:
cd symmetrix/pair_symmetrix
./install.sh ../../lammps

cd ../symmetrix

export TORCH_CMAKE_DIR="$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/share/cmake"
export PYBIND11_CMAKE_DIR="$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pybind11/share/cmake/pybind11"
export CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH="${TORCH_CMAKE_DIR}:${PYBIND11_CMAKE_DIR}"

export CMAKE_GENERATOR="Unix Makefiles"
export CMAKE_ARGS="-DKokkos_ENABLE_HIP=ON \
  -DSYMMETRIX_KOKKOS=ON \
  -DKokkos_ARCH_AMD_GFX942=ON \
  -DCMAKE_HIP_ARCHITECTURES=gfx942 \
  -DCMAKE_C_COMPILER=$(which mpicc) \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_COMPILER=$(which hipcc) \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_STANDARD=20 \
  -DCMAKE_CXX_SCAN_FOR_MODULES=OFF \
  -DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH=${CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH}"

pip install --no-build-isolation . 2>&1 | tee "${ROOT}/symmetrix_build.log"

docs.title: 7. Configure and build LAMMPS
docs.text:
cd ../../lammps
mkdir -p build && cd build

export TORCH_CMAKE_DIR="$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.10/site-packages/torch/share/cmake"
export PYBIND11_CMAKE_DIR="$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pybind11/share/cmake/pybind11"
export CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH="${TORCH_CMAKE_DIR}:${PYBIND11_CMAKE_DIR}"

PREFIX="${PWD}/install"

cmake ../cmake \
  -D CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release \
  -D CMAKE_C_COMPILER="${CC}" \
  -D CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER="$(which hipcc)" \
  -D CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD=20 \
  -D BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON \
  -D BUILD_MPI=ON \
  -D PKG_KOKKOS=ON \
  -D PKG_PYTHON=ON \
  -D PKG_EXTRA-FIX=YES \
  -D FFT=KISS \
  -D Kokkos_ENABLE_OPENMP=ON \
  -D Kokkos_ENABLE_HIP=ON \
  -D SYMMETRIX_KOKKOS=ON \
  -D Kokkos_ENABLE_AGGRESSIVE_VECTORIZATION=ON \
  -D Kokkos_ARCH_AMD_GFX942=ON \
  -D CMAKE_HIP_ARCHITECTURES=gfx942 \
  -D Kokkos_HOST_COMPILER="${KOKKOS_HOST_COMPILER}" \
  -D HIP_HIPCC_EXECUTABLE="$(which hipcc)" \
  -D PKG_MISC=YES \
  -D PKG_DIPOLE=YES \
  -D PKG_REPLICA=YES \
  -D PKG_EXTRA-COMMAND=YES \
  -D PKG_EXTRA-COMPUTE=YES \
  -D PKG_EXTRA-DUMP=YES \
  -D PKG_EXTRA-MOLECULE=YES \
  -D PKG_EXTRA-PAIR=YES \
  -D PKG_DIELECTRIC=YES \
  -D PKG_RIGID=YES \
  -D MKL_INCLUDE_DIR=/tmp \
  -D PKG_ML-MACE=ON \
  -D PKG_USER-MISC=YES \
  -D CRAYPE_LINK_TYPE=dynamic \
  -D CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX="${PREFIX}" \
  -D CMAKE_PREFIX_PATH="${TORCH_CMAKE_DIR};${PYBIND11_CMAKE_DIR}"

make -j 96 2>&1 | tee "${ROOT}/lammps_make.log"
make install-python 2>&1 | tee "${ROOT}/lammps_install_python.log"

Parallel build cores

Adjust -j 96 to the number of CPU cores available on your build node.

docs.title: 8. Verify the build
docs.text:
After a successful build you should see:

Build complete
VENV: /path/to/venvs/allegro_env
LAMMPS binary: .../lammps/build/lmp
LAMMPS shared lib: .../lammps/build/liblammps.so.0

Build logs are saved in ${ROOT}/symmetrix_build.log, ${ROOT}/lammps_make.log, and ${ROOT}/lammps_install_python.log for debugging.

docs.title: LAMMPS Overview
docs.text: LAMMPS (Large-scale Atomic/Molecular Massively Parallel Simulator) is the main MD code used in the group.

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text:
Guide Description Input File Structure Anatomy of a LAMMPS input script

Work in progress

More tutorials (thermostats, barostats, ML potentials, analysis) will be added here.

docs.title: LAMMPS Input File Structure
docs.text: A LAMMPS input script is read top-to-bottom. Commands fall into a few logical sections.

docs.title: Minimal example
docs.text:
# ── 1. Initialization ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
units           metal          # eV, Å, ps, bar
dimension       3
boundary        p p p          # periodic in x, y, z
atom_style      atomic

# ── 2. Geometry ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
read_data       structure.lammps

# ── 3. Interatomic potential ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
pair_style      mace no_domain_decomposition
pair_coeff      * * my_model.model Cu

# ── 4. Settings ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
neighbor        2.0 bin
neigh_modify    every 1 delay 0 check yes

# ── 5. Output ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
thermo          100
thermo_style    custom step temp press etotal vol

dump            1 all custom 100 dump.lammpstrj id type x y z

# ── 6. Run ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
timestep        0.001          # 1 fs in metal units

fix             1 all nvt temp 300.0 300.0 0.1
run             10000

docs.title: 1. Initialization
docs.text: Command Purpose units Sets unit system (metal, real, si, lj, …) boundary Periodic (p) or non-periodic (f/s) per axis atom_style Atom attributes (atomic, charge, full, …)

docs.title: 2. Geometry
docs.text: Command Purpose read_data Read atom positions from a .lammps data file lattice + region + create_atoms Build a lattice from scratch

docs.title: 3. Interatomic potential
docs.text: Command Purpose pair_style Functional form of the potential pair_coeff Parameters / model file kspace_style Long-range electrostatics (Ewald, PPPM)

docs.title: 4. Settings
docs.text: Command Purpose neighbor Skin distance for neighbor list neigh_modify How often to rebuild the neighbor list group Define atom groups for selective fixes/dumps

docs.title: 5. Output
docs.text: Command Purpose thermo Print thermodynamic data every N steps thermo_style custom Choose which quantities to print dump Write trajectory file restart Write binary restart files

docs.title: 6. Run
docs.text: Command Purpose timestep Integration time step fix Apply integrators, thermostats, constraints minimize Energy minimization instead of dynamics run Number of MD steps to execute

docs.title: Common units reference
docs.text: System Energy Length Time Force metal eV Å ps eV/Å real kcal/mol Å fs kcal/(mol·Å) si J m s N lj ε σ — —

docs.title: Python & ASE Overview
docs.text: Python is the primary scripting language in the group. We use it for data analysis, workflow automation, and atomistic simulations via the ASE library.

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text:
Guide Description Basic Commands Python essentials for scientific computing ASE Tutorial Atomic Simulation Environment — structures, calculators, analysis

Work in progress

Guides for NumPy, Matplotlib, and workflow automation will be added.

docs.title: ASE Tutorial
docs.text: ASE (Atomic Simulation Environment) is a Python library for setting up, running, and analyzing atomistic simulations.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: pip install ase

docs.title: Building structures
docs.text:
from ase.build import bulk, molecule, surface
from ase.io import read, write

# FCC bulk crystal
cu = bulk("Cu", "fcc", a=3.615)

# Simple molecule
h2o = molecule("H2O")

# FCC (111) surface, 4 layers, 10 Å vacuum
slab = surface("Cu", (1, 1, 1), layers=4, vacuum=10.0)

# Read from file (POSCAR, xyz, cif, lammps data, …)
atoms = read("POSCAR")
atoms = read("structure.xyz")

# Write to file
write("out.xyz", atoms)
write("POSCAR", atoms)
write("structure.lammps", atoms, specorder=["Cu", "O"])

docs.title: Inspecting structures
docs.text:
print(atoms)                       # summary
print(atoms.get_positions())       # Nx3 array, Å
print(atoms.get_chemical_symbols())
print(atoms.get_cell())            # 3x3 cell matrix
print(atoms.get_volume())          # Å³
print(len(atoms))                  # number of atoms

# Access a single atom
atoms[0].symbol
atoms[0].position
atoms[0].mass

docs.title: Manipulating structures
docs.text:
from ase.build import make_supercell
import numpy as np

# Supercell
supercell = make_supercell(cu, [[2, 0, 0],
                                [0, 2, 0],
                                [0, 0, 2]])

# Repeat shorthand
supercell = cu.repeat([2, 2, 2])

# Rotate
atoms.rotate(45, "z", center="COP")

# Translate
atoms.translate([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])

# Add / remove atoms
from ase import Atom
atoms.append(Atom("H", position=(0, 0, 5)))
del atoms[0]

docs.title: Attaching a calculator
docs.text:
# EMT (built-in, for quick tests with metals)
from ase.calculators.emt import EMT

atoms.calc = EMT()
e = atoms.get_potential_energy()    # eV
f = atoms.get_forces()              # eV/Å
s = atoms.get_stress()              # Voigt, eV/Å³

# LAMMPS calculator
from ase.calculators.lammpsrun import LAMMPS
calc = LAMMPS(pair_style="eam/alloy",
              pair_coeff=["* * Cu.eam.alloy Cu"])
atoms.calc = calc

docs.title: Structure optimization
docs.text:
from ase.optimize import BFGS, FIRE

opt = BFGS(atoms, trajectory="relax.traj")
opt.run(fmax=0.01)    # converge forces below 0.01 eV/Å

# Or with FIRE
opt = FIRE(atoms)
opt.run(fmax=0.01)

docs.title: Reading trajectories
docs.text:
from ase.io import read

# Read all frames
traj = read("relax.traj", index=":")   # returns a list of Atoms

for frame in traj:
    print(frame.get_potential_energy())

# Read last frame only
last = read("relax.traj")

docs.title: Visualisation
docs.text:
from ase.visualize import view

view(atoms)               # opens ASE GUI
view(traj)                # animate trajectory

Headless servers

On HPC clusters without a display, use write("out.png", atoms) or write("out.html", atoms) to produce static/interactive images.

docs.title: Python Basics for Scientific Computing
docs.text: A quick-reference for the Python patterns used most often in the group.

docs.title: NumPy essentials
docs.text:
import numpy as np

# Create arrays
a = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 3.0])
b = np.zeros((3, 3))
c = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)   # 100 evenly spaced points

# Array operations (element-wise)
a * 2
np.sqrt(a)
np.dot(a, a)          # dot product
np.linalg.norm(a)     # vector norm

# Slicing
data = np.loadtxt("file.dat")
x = data[:, 0]        # first column
y = data[:, 1]        # second column
y[y > 0]              # boolean mask

# Save / load
np.savetxt("out.dat", np.column_stack([x, y]))
np.save("array.npy", a)
a = np.load("array.npy")

docs.title: Matplotlib quick plots
docs.text:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.plot(x, y, label="my data")
ax.set_xlabel("x")
ax.set_ylabel("y")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("plot.pdf")
plt.show()

docs.title: File I/O
docs.text:
# Read a plain text file line by line
with open("file.txt") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())

# Write to a file
with open("out.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write(f"value = {42}\n")

# JSON
import json
with open("params.json") as f:
    params = json.load(f)

docs.title: Useful one-liners
docs.text:
# List comprehension
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]

# Enumerate with index
for i, val in enumerate(squares):
    print(i, val)

# Zip two lists
for x, y in zip(list_a, list_b):
    print(x, y)

# f-strings
name = "LAMMPS"
print(f"Running {name} with {96} cores")

docs.title: Virtual environments
docs.text:
# Create
python -m venv myenv

# Activate (Linux/macOS)
source myenv/bin/activate

# Activate (Windows)
myenv\Scripts\activate

# Install packages
pip install numpy matplotlib ase

# Deactivate
deactivate

docs.title: Quantum ESPRESSO Overview
docs.text: Quantum ESPRESSO is used for DFT calculations (plane-wave basis, pseudopotentials).

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text:
Guide Description Input File Structure pw.x input namelist reference

Work in progress

Guides for ph.x, cp.x, and post-processing tools will be added.

docs.title: Quantum ESPRESSO Input File (pw.x)
docs.text: The main QE executable pw.x reads a single input file divided into namelists and cards.

docs.title: Minimal SCF example
docs.text:
&CONTROL
  calculation  = 'scf'
  prefix       = 'silicon'
  outdir       = './tmp'
  pseudo_dir   = './pseudos'
/

&SYSTEM
  ibrav        = 2
  celldm(1)    = 10.26        ! lattice parameter in Bohr
  nat          = 2
  ntyp         = 1
  ecutwfc      = 60.0         ! plane-wave cutoff in Ry
  ecutrho      = 480.0        ! charge-density cutoff in Ry
/

&ELECTRONS
  conv_thr     = 1.0e-8
/

ATOMIC_SPECIES
 Si  28.086  Si.pbe-n-rrkjus_psl.1.0.0.UPF

ATOMIC_POSITIONS {crystal}
 Si  0.00  0.00  0.00
 Si  0.25  0.25  0.25

K_POINTS {automatic}
 8 8 8  0 0 0

docs.title: &CONTROL
docs.text: Parameter Description calculation 'scf', 'relax', 'vc-relax', 'nscf', 'bands' prefix Label for output files outdir Directory for temporary files pseudo_dir Directory containing pseudopotential files tprnfor Print forces (.true./.false.) tstress Print stress tensor

docs.title: &SYSTEM
docs.text: Parameter Description ibrav Bravais lattice index (0 = free, read from CELL_PARAMETERS) celldm(1) Lattice parameter in Bohr (used when ibrav ≠ 0) nat Number of atoms ntyp Number of atomic species ecutwfc Kinetic energy cutoff for wavefunctions (Ry) ecutrho Cutoff for charge density (Ry); typically 8–12 × ecutwfc occupations 'smearing' for metals; 'fixed' for insulators smearing 'mp' (Methfessel-Paxton), 'mv', 'gaussian' degauss Smearing width in Ry nspin 1 non-spin, 2 spin-polarized

docs.title: &ELECTRONS
docs.text: Parameter Description conv_thr SCF convergence threshold on total energy (Ry) mixing_beta Charge mixing parameter (default 0.7; reduce for hard cases) diagonalization 'david' (default) or 'cg'

docs.title: &IONS (for relax / vc-relax)
docs.text: Parameter Description ion_dynamics 'bfgs' (default) or 'damp'

docs.title: &CELL (for vc-relax)
docs.text: Parameter Description cell_dynamics 'bfgs' press Target pressure in kbar

docs.title: Cards reference
docs.text: Card Description ATOMIC_SPECIES Species name, mass, pseudopotential filename ATOMIC_POSITIONS Atom positions; units: {crystal}, {angstrom}, {bohr} K_POINTS k-point mesh; {automatic} for Monkhorst-Pack CELL_PARAMETERS Lattice vectors when ibrav = 0; units {angstrom} or {bohr}

docs.title: VASP Overview
docs.text: VASP (Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package) is a plane-wave DFT code.

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text:
Guide Description Input Files INCAR, POSCAR, KPOINTS, POTCAR reference

Work in progress

Guides for NEB, phonons, and hybrid functionals will be added.

docs.title: VASP Input Files
docs.text: A VASP calculation requires four input files in the same directory.

docs.title: File overview
docs.text: File Role INCAR Calculation parameters (what to compute, how) POSCAR Crystal structure (cell + atomic positions) KPOINTS k-point sampling POTCAR Pseudopotentials / PAW datasets (concatenated)

docs.title: INCAR
docs.text:
Controls every aspect of the calculation.

# Basic SCF
SYSTEM  = Si bulk
ISTART  = 0       # 0 = new calculation
ICHARG  = 2       # 2 = superposition of atomic charges

# Electronic minimization
ENCUT   = 520     # plane-wave cutoff in eV
EDIFF   = 1E-6    # SCF convergence criterion (eV)
ALGO    = Fast    # RMM-DIIS + Davidson
NELM    = 100     # max SCF steps

# k-point smearing (metals)
ISMEAR  = 1       # Methfessel-Paxton
SIGMA   = 0.2     # smearing width in eV

# Output
LORBIT  = 11      # write DOSCAR and projected DOS
LWAVE   = .FALSE. # do not write WAVECAR
LCHARG  = .FALSE. # do not write CHGCAR

docs.title: Common INCAR tags
docs.text: Tag Values Description ENCUT (eV) Plane-wave cutoff EDIFF 1E-5 to 1E-8 SCF energy tolerance EDIFFG negative (eV/Å) Force tolerance for relaxation NSW integer Max ionic steps (0 = single-point) IBRION 2 Ionic relaxation algorithm (2 = CG) ISIF 2/3 2 = relax ions; 3 = relax ions + cell ISMEAR 1/-5 1 = MP smearing (metals); -5 = tetrahedron (insulators) SIGMA (eV) Smearing width GGA PE PBE functional LDAU .TRUE. Enable DFT+U NPAR integer Parallelization over bands

docs.title: POSCAR
docs.text:
Si FCC bulk
1.0
  2.715  2.715  0.000
  0.000  2.715  2.715
  2.715  0.000  2.715
Si
2
Direct
  0.000  0.000  0.000
  0.250  0.250  0.250

Line-by-line:

  1. Comment / system name
  2. Universal scale factor 3–5. Lattice vectors (Å)
  3. Element names (must match POTCAR order)
  4. Number of atoms per species
  5. Direct (fractional) or Cartesian 9+. Atomic positions

docs.title: POTCAR
docs.text:
Concatenate PAW datasets for each species in the same order as POSCAR:

cat $VASP_PP_PATH/PAW_PBE/Si/POTCAR > POTCAR
# for binary: cat .../Cu/POTCAR .../O/POTCAR > POTCAR

Order matters

The species order in POTCAR must exactly match POSCAR line 6.
